sentences.base_language: One morning, Kenji woke up with a headache and a stomachache, and felt very tired.
sentences.target_language: ある朝、健二は頭と胃が痛くて、とても疲れた感じがした。
sentences.romanized: Aru asa, Kenji wa atama to i ga itakute, totemo tsukareta kanji ga shita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜が痛い** = to have pain in ~: 頭**が**痛い (head hurts), 胃**が**痛い (stomach hurts). The body part is the grammatical subject marked with **が**. Compare: 頭**を**打った (hit one's head — action), 頭**が**痛い (head is aching — state).
**〜くて** — te-form of **い**-adjectives: 痛**くて** (hurts and…). The te-form of い-adjectives (drop い, add くて) connects clauses, implying cause or sequence: 忙し**くて** 眠れない (so busy can't sleep), 寒**くて** 外に出たくない (cold, so don't want to go out).
**感じがした** = had a feeling of: **感じがする** (present) / **感じがした** (past). A very natural pattern: 変な**感じがする** (feels weird), 懐かしい**感じがした** (had a nostalgic feeling).

sentences.base_language: Lately he had been working a lot and wasn't sleeping well.
sentences.target_language: 最近、ずっとたくさん働いていて、よく眠れていなかった。
sentences.romanized: Saikin, zutto takusan hataraite ite, yoku nemurete inakatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ていて** — te-form of the 〜ている progressive: 働い**ていて** (working, and…). This stacks the progressive aspect onto the te-form connector: 走っ**ていて** 転んだ (was running and fell), 疲れ**ていて** 眠れなかった (was tired and couldn't sleep).
**眠れていなかった** — potential + progressive negative past: 眠**れる** (can sleep) → 眠れ**ていない** (has not been able to sleep) → 眠れ**ていなかった** (had not been able to sleep). The 〜ている layer adds the ongoing/habitual dimension — he hadn't been managing to sleep, as a sustained situation. Compare: 眠れなかった (couldn't sleep — single occasion).

sentences.base_language: He decided to go to the hospital near his home.
sentences.target_language: 彼は家の近くにある病院に行くことにした。
sentences.romanized: Kare wa ie no chikaku ni aru byōin ni iku koto ni shita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことにする** = to decide to do: 行く**ことにした** (decided to go). This pattern expresses a deliberate, personal decision. Compare: 行く**ことになった** (it turned out/was decided that he would go — not necessarily his own choice; external arrangement). **〜ことにする** emphasises the agent's own will; **〜ことになる** emphasises that things came to be that way.
**近くにある病院** — modifying a noun with a verb clause: **近くにある** (that exists nearby) modifies **病院** (hospital). In Japanese, modifying clauses precede the noun they describe: 昨日**買った** 本 (the book I bought yesterday), おいしい**と言われている** レストラン (the restaurant said to be delicious).

sentences.base_language: He washed his face and got dressed, but felt too weak to eat breakfast.
sentences.target_language: 顔を洗って着替えたが、朝食を食べるには体が弱りすぎていた。
sentences.romanized: Kao wo aratte kigaeta ga, chōshoku wo taberu ni wa karada ga yowari sugite ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜すぎる** = to be too ~, to do too much: 弱り**すぎる** (to be too weak), 食べ**すぎる** (to eat too much), 難し**すぎる** (too difficult). Attach **すぎる** to the verb stem (masu-stem) or to the stem of い-adjectives (drop い): 忙し**すぎる**, 高**すぎる**.
**〜には** = for the purpose of, in order to: 食べる**には** (in order to eat / for eating). This use of **には** frames the condition or requirement: 合格する**には** 勉強が必要だ (in order to pass, study is necessary). Compare: **ために** (for the sake of, purpose): 健康のため**に** 運動する (exercise for health). **〜には** focuses on the prerequisite condition; **ために** emphasises purpose or benefit.

sentences.base_language: The hospital was two subway stops from his apartment.
sentences.target_language: 病院はアパートから地下鉄で二駅のところにあった。
sentences.romanized: Byōin wa apāto kara chikatetsu de ni-eki no tokoro ni atta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜から地下鉄で二駅** = two subway stops from ~: **から** (from) + **地下鉄で** (by subway, using **で** for means of transport) + **二駅** (two stations). Distance in stops: **三駅先** (three stops ahead), **一駅手前** (one stop before).
**〜のところにある** = to be at a place of / to be located at: 二駅**のところにある** (located at the point of two stops). **ところ** (place/point) is used abstractly here to indicate position. Compare: 角**のところに** ある (at the corner), 少し先**のところに** ある (a little further ahead). This pattern is more natural than simply saying **に** alone when describing a relative location.

sentences.base_language: At the reception, a nurse asked him: "How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: 受付で、看護師が「どのようなご用件でしょうか？」と聞いた。
sentences.romanized: Uketsuke de, kangoshi ga 'Dono yō na go-yōken deshō ka?' to kiita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**ご用件** — honorific prefix **ご** (御) added to 用件 (business/matter). **ご** (from 御, read go) is used before Sino-Japanese words: **ご**用件, **ご**家族, **ご**連絡. Compare: **お** is used before native Japanese words: **お**名前, **お**水, **お**仕事. Knowing which prefix to use (ご vs お) is essential for polite speech.
**〜でしょうか** — polite conjecture/question: より丁寧な「〜ですか」. **でしょう** is the formal/polite equivalent of **だろう** (conjecture). In service contexts, **でしょうか** is the standard polite question form: ご注文は何に**なさいますか** (very formal) vs 何に**しますか** (casual). **どのような** (what kind of) is the formal equivalent of **どんな**.

sentences.base_language: He replied: "My head and stomach hurt, and I feel completely exhausted."
sentences.target_language: 「頭と胃が痛くて、完全にへとへとです」と答えた。
sentences.romanized: 'Atama to i ga itakute, kanzen ni hetoheto desu' to kotaeta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**へとへと** — a mimetic word (擬態語, gitaigo) expressing total physical exhaustion: へとへとだ (I'm wiped out). Japanese has an extensive system of such sound-symbolic words. Compare: **くたくた** (exhausted from overwork), **ぐったり** (limp/drained), **へろへろ** (wobbly with fatigue). Each has a slightly different feel — **へとへと** emphasises that every last bit of energy is gone.
**〜と答えた** — quotation with **と**: the particle **と** marks the content of speech or thought: 「…」**と** 言った (said that…), 「…」**と** 思った (thought that…), 「…」**と** 答えた (answered that…). The quote can be a full sentence or fragment. In formal writing, 「 」are used for direct speech; in indirect speech, plain form + **と** + reporting verb.

sentences.base_language: A doctor examined him and asked: "Do you exercise regularly? How is your diet?"
sentences.target_language: 医師が彼を診て、「運動は定期的にしていますか？食事はどうですか？」と尋ねた。
sentences.romanized: Ishi ga kare wo mite, 'Undō wa teikiteki ni shite imasu ka? Shokuji wa dō desu ka?' to tazuneta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**診る** (miru) = to examine (medically) — distinct from **見る** (to see/look). The kanji 診 contains 言 (words) + 人 (person) under 言 — suggesting the act of assessing a person. Common medical vocabulary: **診る** (examine), **診察** (medical examination), **処方する** (prescribe), **症状** (symptoms).
**定期的に** — adverb from noun + 的 + に: 定期 (regular intervals) + **的** (adjectivizing suffix) + **に** (adverbial に). This **〜的に** pattern converts nouns into adverbs: 積極**的に** (actively), 具体**的に** (concretely), 効果**的に** (effectively). Very productive in formal and academic Japanese.

sentences.base_language: He admitted he had been eating a lot of junk food and barely resting.
sentences.target_language: ジャンクフードばかり食べていて、ほとんど休んでいなかったと認めた。
sentences.romanized: Jankufūdo bakari tabete ite, hotondo yasunde inakatta to mitometa.
literal: ジャンクフードばかり
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ばかり** = only, nothing but: ジャンクフード**ばかり** 食べる (eat nothing but junk food). **ばかり** attaches to nouns or verb stems and expresses exclusivity, often with a negative nuance: テレビ**ばかり** 見ている (does nothing but watch TV), 文句**ばかり** 言う (does nothing but complain). Compare: **だけ** (only — neutral) vs **ばかり** (only — tends to imply excessiveness).
**ほとんど〜ない** = hardly/barely: **ほとんど** 休んでいなかった (was barely resting). **ほとんど** with a negative verb = 'hardly': ほとんど**食べない** (barely eats), ほとんど**眠れない** (can hardly sleep). Without negation, ほとんど means 'almost all/mostly': ほとんどの人 (almost all people), ほとんど終わった (almost finished).

sentences.base_language: The doctor said: "You need more rest and a better diet. Drink plenty of water."
sentences.target_language: 「もっとしっかり休んで、食事も改善してください。水もたくさん飲んでください」と医師は言った。
sentences.romanized: 'Motto shikkari yasunde, shokuji mo kaizen shite kudasai. Mizu mo takusan nonde kudasai' to ishi wa itta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**しっかり** = properly, firmly, thoroughly — an adverb implying that something is done with care and substance: しっかり**休む** (rest properly), しっかり**食べる** (eat well), しっかり**勉強する** (study hard). It conveys effort and sufficiency. Compare: **ちゃんと** (properly, as expected — focuses on meeting a standard), **きちんと** (neatly, correctly — focuses on order). All three overlap but **しっかり** adds a sense of robustness.
**改善する** = to improve (a situation or habit): 食事を**改善する** (improve one's diet), 状況を**改善する** (improve a situation). This is a Sino-Japanese compound (改 reform + 善 good) used for deliberate, systematic improvement. Compare: よくなる (get better — natural/spontaneous improvement without deliberate effort).

sentences.base_language: He felt a little embarrassed, but also relieved to know it wasn't serious.
sentences.target_language: 少し恥ずかしい気持ちになったが、深刻ではないとわかってほっとした。
sentences.romanized: Sukoshi hazukashii kimochi ni natta ga, shinkoku dewa nai to wakatte hotto shita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**気持ちになる** = to come to feel, to find oneself feeling: 恥ずかしい**気持ちになった** (came to feel embarrassed). **気持ち** (feeling/mood) + **になる** (to become) creates a nuanced expression of a feeling that arises or comes over one. Compare: 恥ずかしく**なった** (became embarrassed — more direct) vs 恥ずかしい**気持ちになった** (found oneself with a feeling of embarrassment — slightly more reflective).
**ほっとする** = to feel relieved — a very natural Japanese expression for relief: **ほっとした** (felt relieved), **ほっとした** 表情 (a relieved expression). The **ほっ** is an onomatopoeic element suggesting a breath released. Compare: **安心する** (feel at ease — more cognitive reassurance), **ほっとする** (physical/emotional release of tension).

sentences.base_language: He left the hospital and found a grocery store nearby.
sentences.target_language: 病院を出て、近くにスーパーがあるのを見つけた。
sentences.romanized: Byōin wo dete, chikaku ni sūpā ga aru no wo mitsuketa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**病院を出る** = to leave the hospital: **出る** (to exit) takes the place you leave with **を**: 家**を**出る (leave home), 駅**を**出る (leave the station), 部屋**を**出る (leave the room). Compare: **から出る** (come out from) and **を出る** (leave) — they overlap, but **を出る** is the standard expression for departing a place.
**〜のを見つけた** = found that ~, noticed that ~: スーパーがある**のを** 見つけた (found that there was a supermarket). The nominalizer **の** turns the clause スーパーがある into a noun, making it the object of 見つける. This pattern is very common: 鍵が落ちている**のを** 見た (saw that the key had fallen), 彼が来た**のを** 知らなかった (didn't know he had come).

sentences.base_language: He bought vegetables, fish, and fruit — no snacks this time.
sentences.target_language: 野菜、魚、果物を買った。今回はお菓子は買わなかった。
sentences.romanized: Yasai, sakana, kudamono wo katta. Konkai wa okashi wa kawanakatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**今回** (this time) vs **今度** (this time / next time): **今回** (今 now + 回 occasion) refers specifically to the current instance or this particular case: **今回**の件 (this matter this time). **今度** is more ambiguous — it can mean 'this time' or 'next time' depending on context: **今度**来たとき (when you come next time) vs **今度**はうまくやる (I'll do it right this time).
**お菓子は買わなかった** — the **は** (topic) on **お菓子** creates a contrastive nuance: as for snacks — did not buy (implying he bought other things). The double **は** structure (今回**は** … お菓子**は**) stacks contrasts: this time (not other times), snacks (not the other things he bought). This contrastive use of **は** is one of the most expressive tools in Japanese.

sentences.base_language: Back home, he made a simple soup with vegetables and chicken.
sentences.target_language: 家に帰って、野菜とチキンを使ったシンプルなスープを作った。
sentences.romanized: Ie ni kaette, yasai to chikin wo tsukatta shinpuru na sūpu wo tsukutta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜を使ったスープ** — relative clause modifying a noun: 野菜を使っ**た**スープ (a soup that uses/used vegetables). In Japanese, modifying clauses always precede the noun: 昨日**作った** スープ (the soup made yesterday), 体に**いい** スープ (a soup that is good for the body). The verb in the modifying clause takes plain form (present or past) regardless of the tense of the main clause.
**帰る** vs **戻る**: **帰る** = to return home (to one's base or residence): 家に**帰る** (go home). **戻る** = to return (to a previous location, not necessarily home): 席に**戻る** (return to one's seat), 元の場所に**戻る** (return to the original place). Using **帰る** for going home is standard and preferred.

sentences.base_language: While cooking, he called his friend and told her about the doctor's visit.
sentences.target_language: 料理しながら、友達に電話して病院のことを話した。
sentences.romanized: Ryōri shinagara, tomodachi ni denwa shite byōin no koto wo hanashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながら** = while doing: 料理し**ながら** (while cooking). **ながら** attaches to the masu-stem of a verb and expresses two simultaneous actions. The subject must be the same for both actions: 音楽を聴き**ながら** 勉強する (study while listening to music). Compare: **〜ている間に** (while ~ is happening — the background action has duration and the main action fits within it; subjects can differ): 彼が寝て**いる間に** 出かけた (left while he was sleeping).
**〜のことを話す** = to talk about ~: 病院**のことを** 話した (talked about the hospital / what happened at the hospital). **〜のこと** adds the nuance of 'the matter of' or 'the story about' — it nominalizes a topic for discussion. Compare: 病院**について** 話す (talk about the hospital — more analytical/informational) vs 病院**のことを** 話す (tell about what happened at the hospital — more personal/narrative).

sentences.base_language: His friend laughed and said: "I always told you to take better care of yourself!"
sentences.target_language: 友達は笑って、「いつも体を大切にしなさいって言ってたでしょ！」と言った。
sentences.romanized: Tomodachi wa waratte, 'Itsumo karada wo taisetsu ni shinasai tte itteta desho!' to itta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜なさい** = command (soft imperative): 大切にし**なさい** (take care of yourself — imperative). **〜なさい** attaches to the masu-stem and sounds like a firm but caring instruction — common from parents, teachers, or close friends. More forceful than **〜てください** (polite request) but less harsh than the plain imperative **〜ろ/〜れ**. **体を大切にする** (take good care of one's body / treat one's body with care) — a very useful fixed expression.
**〜って言ってたでしょ** — casual reporting with confirmation: **〜って** is the casual spoken equivalent of **〜と** (quotation marker). **言ってた** (was saying) = 言っていた (contracted). **でしょ** (right? / didn't I?) adds the nuance of 'I told you so' — seeking acknowledgment. This whole expression 「〜って言ってたでしょ！」 is a very authentic, colloquial 'I told you so!'

sentences.base_language: After eating, he felt much better — his energy was returning.
sentences.target_language: 食べた後、ずっと気分がよくなってきた。エネルギーが戻ってきている感じがした。
sentences.romanized: Tabeta ato, zutto kibun ga yoku natte kita. Enerugī ga modotte kite iru kanji ga shita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくる** = to do something and it comes toward the present / a change begins and approaches now: よくなって**きた** (has been getting better — the improvement has been happening and continues to now). **〜てくる** expresses a change or action that starts in the past and moves toward the present moment. Compare: よくなって**いく** (is getting better and will continue to — moving away from now, into the future).
**戻ってきている** — combining direction and progressive: 戻って**きて**いる (is in the process of coming back/returning). The **きて** provides the directional nuance (coming back, toward the present) and **いる** adds the progressive aspect (the process is ongoing). This kind of stacking of aspectual and directional auxiliaries is characteristic of natural Japanese.

sentences.base_language: He lay down and rested for a couple of hours.
sentences.target_language: 横になって、二、三時間ほど休んだ。
sentences.romanized: Yoko ni natte, ni, san-jikan hodo yasunda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**横になる** = to lie down: 横 (horizontal/sideways) + **になる** (to become). A very natural and common expression: **横になって** 休む (rest by lying down), **横になっても** いいですか (may I lie down?). Compare: **寝る** (to sleep/lie down — more about actual sleeping), **横になる** (to lie down — emphasises the position, not necessarily sleep).
**二、三時間ほど** = about two or three hours: **二、三〜** (two or three ~) is a natural approximation, equivalent to 'a couple of' in English. **ほど** (approximately, about) softens the number further: 一時間**ほど** (about an hour), 五分**ほど** (about five minutes). Compare: **くらい**/**ごろ** (approximately — for times and amounts); **ほど** tends to be slightly more formal.

sentences.base_language: By evening, his mood had completely improved and he felt calm and content.
sentences.target_language: 夕方になるころには、気分がすっかりよくなり、穏やかで満足した気持ちになっていた。
sentences.romanized: Yūgata ni naru koro ni wa, kibun ga sukkari yoku nari, odayaka de manzoku shita kimochi ni natte ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ころには** = by the time ~: 夕方になる**ころには** (by the time evening came). **ころ** (approximate time) + **には** (topic/frame marker) sets a time boundary: 三時**ごろには** 終わるはず (should be done by around three). Compare: 夕方**に** (at evening — specific point) vs 夕方**ころ** (around evening — approximate) vs 夕方**ころには** (by evening — with expectation or result).
**すっかり** = completely, entirely (implies thorough change): **すっかり** よくなった (got completely better), **すっかり** 忘れた (completely forgot). **すっかり** often implies that a previous state is entirely gone or replaced. Compare: **完全に** (completely — neutral, formal) and **すっかり** (completely — implies a felt, thorough transformation, often positive).

sentences.base_language: He promised himself to take better care of his body from now on.
sentences.target_language: これからはもっと体を大切にしようと、自分に誓った。
sentences.romanized: Korekara wa motto karada wo taisetsu ni shiyō to, jibun ni chikatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜しようと** — volitional + quotation marker: 大切にし**ようと** (vowing that he would take care of). The volitional form (〜よう/〜おう) + **と** is used to quote an intention or mental resolution: 頑張ろう**と** 思った (thought he would try hard), 諦めまい**と** 誓った (vowed not to give up). This pattern captures the inner voice or decision.
**これから** vs **今後**: **これから** (from now, going forward — spoken, immediate resolve): **これから**は気をつける (I'll be careful from now on). **今後** (henceforth — more formal, written): **今後**とも よろしくお願いします (I ask for your continued support). **これからは** at the start of a sentence signals a personal resolution or turning point — very common in reflective, first-person narrative.
